.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4085:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:305:      "content": "Title: 1320380\n\nURL Source: https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/1320380\n\nNumber of Pages: 20\n\nMarkdown Content:\nAdhesion and debonding kinetics of photovoltaic  encapsulation \n\n# in moist environments \n\n## Fernand o D. Novoa 1, David C. Miller 2 and Reinhold H. Dauskardt 1*  \n\n> 1Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Stanford University, Stanford, CA 94305 -2205, USA\n> 2Nati onal Center for Photovoltaics, NREL, Golden, CO, USA\n\n# ABSTRACT \n\nDebonding of phot ovoltaic (PV) encapsulation in moist environments is frequently reported but presently \n\nnot well understood or quanti fi ed. Temperature cycling, moisture, and mechanical loads oft en cause loss of \n\nencapsulation adhesion and  interfacial debonding, initially fa cilitating back -re fl ectance and reduced \n\nelectrical current, but ultimately leading to internal  corrosion and loss of module functionality. To \n\ninvestigate the effects of temperat ure ( T) and relative humidity (RH) on the  kinetics of encapsulation \n\ndebonding,  we developed a mechanics -based technique to measure encapsulation debond energy  and \n\ndebond growth rates in a chamber of controlled environment. The debond energy decreased from  2.15 to \n\n1.75 kJ m 2 in  poly(ethylene -co -vinyl acetate) (EVA) and from 0.67 to 0. 52 kJ m 2 in polyvinyl butyral when  T\n\nincreased from 25 to 50° C and 20 to 40° C, respectively. The debond growth rates of EVA increased up to \n\n1000 -fold with small increases of  T (10° C)  and RH (15%). To elucidate the mechanisms of environmental \n\ndebonding, we  developed a fracture -kinetics model, where  the viscoelastic relaxation processes at the \n\ndebonding -tip are used to predict debond growth. The model and techniques  constitute the  fundamental \n\nbasis for developing accelerated aging tests and long -term reliabil ity predictions for PV  encapsulation.  \n\n> KEYWORDS encapsulation debonding; interfacial adhesion; moisture; delamination; durability\n\n# 1.  INTRODUCTION  Photovoltaic (PV) encapsulatio n is arguably the most important structural \n\ncomponent of PV modules. It holds the  module together and protects it from aggressive  fi eld  environments \n\nthat include moisture, external mechanical  loads, and temperature cycles[1,2] . Guaranteeing the  long -term \n\n(up to 25 years) interfacial stability of PV encapsulation is currently a remarkable challenge; preventing \n\ninterfacial debonding of similar  fi lm technologies in othe  industries has so far proven dif fi cult and costly[3]. \n\nUpon  encapsulation debonding, additio nal optical interfaces are  generated within the module, resulting in \n\nlocal backre fl ection and reduced electrical current. Following  debonding, several corrosion processes of the \n\nmodule  components can accelerate dramatically b ecause of direct  contact with t he environment[4,5]. PV \n\nencapsulation  debonding can therefore be a crucial limiting factor for  the yield and reliability of PV modules, \n\neventually risking  loss of function[6,7]. Although the effect of hot and humid  fi eld e nvironments on \n\nencapsulation degra dation is often  reported[4,6,8], the fundamental kinetic processes that  lead to \n\ndebonding have not yet been characterized . Debonding in similar structures, however, has been shown  to \n\ndepend strongly on moisture[9,10], temp erature[11,12],  mechanical stress[ 13], and their synergistic \n\ninteractions  [14]. The effects of these variables on PV encapsulation  debonding are important not only to \n\nassess long -term  encapsulation reliability, which may limit module lifetime, but also to  develop tests and \n\nprotocols to imp rove  encapsulation formulation(s) and manufacturing (surfacepreparation) procedures. \n\nEncapsulation debonding occurs when the strain energy release rate, G (J m 2), or debond driving force,  is \n\nequal to the critical debond  energy (adhesion) at the encapsulat ion interface, G c. However, debonding  in the \n\npresence of active environments can occur at debond -driving forces below G c, because of the  effects of \n\nmoisture and temperature on the kinetic processes that occur at the debonding -tip, such as  viscoelastic rela xation, chemical reactions, and water transport. Encapsulation adhesion has been  previously studied \n\nusing peeling -based techniques includ ing the 180°[15 –17], 90°“T” [1,18,19], and  load -controlled 45°peel \n\ntests [20]. These techniques presently dominate th e study of encapsulation  and backsheet adhesion, but the \n\nresulting peel -strength is not an interfacial property and cannot be  used to com pare between dissimilar \n\nmaterials. Peel -strength results include macroscopic plastic  deformation of the encapsulation m aterial and \n\nthe applied handle layer (tape used to perform the  peel -test), which are difficult to quantify[21 –23], \n\nunrelated to the adhes ive mechanism, and irrelevant  to infield debonding. Practically, deviation of peel angle \n\nfrom its nominal value as well  as cavitation  (because of the large separation angle) also reduce \n\ncomparability between materials. More importantly,  debonding of viscoe lastic films is strongly affected by \n\nthe debonding -tip deformation processes  [9,10,24], which are not well represented  by the various peeling \n\ntechniques. \n\nOther mechanical methods  that have been applied to study PV encapsulation attachment interfaces includ e\n\nthe overlap shear  test[2,25], rotational torque[26], compressive shear test[27], wedge test[28 –30], and the \n\nblister  test  [31,32]. The former methods examine the strength of the attachment layer rather than interfacial \n\nadhesion to the relevant substrate.  The overlap shear test has long been known to be limited by the \n\nmechanical compliance (displacement per unit load) of  the specimen handles[33 –35]. The compressive \n\nshear test or double -lap shear[33] specimen geometries can be used to avoid this limitation.  While  insight \n\nmay be gained by analogy using the former methods, the results may not be directly related to  the \n\nfundam ental mechanic and kinetic processes of debonding. The wedge and blister methods do  instead make \n\nuse of fundamental fracture mechanics pri nciples, but they have not yet been  demonstrated using PV \n\nmaterials. The wedge test, however, may not be capable of de termining the  initial (unaged) G c in strongly \n\nadhered systems, like PV encapsulation. \n\nThe effect of simulated  environments on encapsulatio n adhesion has been mostly studied by replicating \n\ntests and sequences  from the module qualification protocols[36,37].  The “UV exposure,” “thermal cycling,” \n\nand “humidity  freeze” tests simulate a brief field exposure (months to a few years at best), whereas  the \n\nconditions  applied in “damp heat” test greatly exceed those encountered in the application [38]. The \n\nqualificati on  tests at best query infant mortality failure modes and do not of themselves investigate the rate \n\nof  degradation. The currently available  aging treatments, as well as the available adhesion tests, provide \n\nlimited insight on the kinetic processes of debon ding and their dependence on mechanical stress,  moisture \n\nand T. For example, softening of the encapsulant (lower E) can occur near the tran sition or  melting \n\ntemperatures, which can significantly reduce encapsulation adhesion and accelerate debonding. \n\nKinet ics -based testing protocols are therefore urgently needed not only to assess encapsulation  reliability \n\nand durability in field environments  but also to define objectives for encapsulation  formulation and \n\nmanufacturing (surface preparation) procedures. \n\nIn  this study, we develop a quantitative characterization  technique to measure the effects of mechanical \n\nstress,  T, and moisture on the debond energy and debond kinetics  of two encapsulation materials used in PV \n\nmodules: poly  (ethylene -co -vinyl acetate) (EVA)  and polyvinyl butyral  (PVB). To measure debond energy, we \n\nfabricated single  cantilever beam debond (SCB) specimens (Section 3.1)  and modi fi ed a well -known SCB test \n\nmetrology (Sections  3.2 and 3.3), where debonding is studied as the propagation  of an inter facial crack[39]. \n\nTo en sure debonding of the  glass/EVA interface, we describe a novel temperature gradient method. To \n\nmeasure encapsulation debond growth rate  (Section 3.4), d a/ dt, we applied a load -relaxation technique  to \n\nthe SCB debond specimens in an en vironment of controlled  temperature,  T, and relative humidity, RH. The \n\ntechnique allowed debond growth rates, d a/ dt, to be accurately  quanti fi ed (~10 4 m s 1 to 10 8) in terms of the \n\ndebonddriving force,  G, and the viscoelastic relaxation processes  at the deb onding -tip that depend  on temperature ( T) and relative humidity (RH). The synergistic effect of UV -light is not  examined in this study. \n\nTensile specimens were also fabricated (Section 3.5) to quantify the effect of temperature on  encapsulation \n\nstiffness, w hich we used to rationa lize the  debond -growth data. Debonding kinetics was modeled using \n\nviscoelastic fracture -kinetics (Section 4), including the softening effect of T and RH on the encapsulation \n\nmodulus.  The model provides a more fundamental understandin g of  the effects of the  environment on \n\nencapsulation debonding.  The techniques and model can be used to develop accelerated testing protocols, \n\nevaluate the adoption of new encapsulations, and make long -term reliability predictions. \n\n# 2. MATERIALS AND METHODS \n\n2.1. Encapsulation  SCB debond specimens \n\nIn order to fabricate representative encapsulation specimens, a 0.45 -mm layer of PV encapsulation (EVA or \n\nPVB) was laminated between a 5.5 -mm -thick glass substrate (low iron -containing, soda -lime  fl oat glass, as \n\nin  the  PV industry)  and a 3.17 -mm -thick titanium plate (6 Al -4 V, Grade -5), as shown in Figure 1a. Prior to \n\nlamination,  the surfaces of the Ti plate and the glass (Sn -de fi cient side)  were carefully cleaned, including \n\nbuf fi ng with pumice  powder, washing with a n aqueous detergent (Billco, Billco  Manufacturing Inc.), rinsing \n\nwith deionized water, and rinsing with isopropyl alcohol. The lamination step was performed in a \n\ncommercial instrument (Model LM -404,  Astropower Inc.) at 145° C for 8 min  under applied pressur e of 1 \n\natm. During lamination, the specimen components  were  fi xed relative to one another using Kapton tape and \n\nwere surrounded with a glass frame to improve thickness  uniformity at the edges. Two parallel cuts (12 -mm \n\napart)  were made through the full thic kness of the Ti plate and the  encapsulation layer with a high -speed \n\ntungsten carbide saw  and a razor blade, respectively (Figure 1b) .\n\nThe elastic beam material (Ti here) was chosen to  maximize elastic cantilever de fl ection  Δ y (m), as given by \n\nwhere  l, h,σy and  εy are the length, thickness, yield stress,  and yield strain of the beam, respectively, and  Gc is \n\nthe encapsulation debond energy, which is unknown before the  debond measurement, but is independent of \n\nbeam material . To maximize Δ y, we calculated the product  σyεy2 for several  candidate beam materials (in \n\nMPa), including steel (0.023),  Al (0.019), poly(methyl methacrylate) (0.039) and Ti  (0.047). We used Ti \n\nbeams in this study but some polymer  beams, such as poly(methy l methacrylate), can be sometimes easier \n\nto bond to the organic PV encapsulation[24]. \n\n2.2. SCB debond test metrology \n\nThe SCB specimen described in Section 3.1 was  fi xe d to a  rigid testing table with screw clamps. A loading tab \n\nwith a  ruby bearing was bonde d to one end of the Ti beam, and  the tab was then connected to an adhesion \n\ntest system  (Delaminator, DTS, Menlo Park, CA) consisting of a linear  actuator (displacem ent -controlled, 0.1 \n\nμm resolution) in  series with a high -resolution mechanical load cell  fi xed to  a high -stiffness frame. The \n\ndisplacement of the linear actuator and the mechanical load were monitored every 0.1 s. \n\nThe actuator was retracted (developing  a tensile force,  P) to  initiate encapsulation debonding, until the \n\ndebond  length (distance from the loading tab to the debondingtip) was ~15 mm. The Ti beam in this \n\ncon fi guration can  be treated as a single cantilever beam  fi xed at the  debonding -tip [39]. T he mechanical \n\ncomplianc e of the  SCB specimen (40 –60  μm/N) was much larger than that  of the testing system (~0.5 \n\nμm/N), which allowed for  accurate debond energy measurements. A minimum beam  thickness (~3 mm here) \n\nwas required to avoid plastic  deformation of  the beam due to bending stress during the  debonding \n\nexperiment. \n\nThe SCB specimen was mechanically loaded at a  constant displacement rate to propagate debonding for a few additional millimeters and then immediately unloaded  to prevent further debonding.  The \n\nloading -unloading  process was repeated for several  cycles at increasing  debond -lengths, adjusting the \n\nactuator displacement rate  to keep the local deformation rate (at the debonding -tip)  constant (refer to \n\nSection 3.5). At each debonding cycle,  the mechanical compliance (displacement per unit load)  of the  SCB \n\nspecimen,  C, and the mechanical load at the  onset of debonding,  Pc (where the load -displacement curve \n\nbecomes non -linear) were recorded. \n\nThe debond driving -force,  G (J m -2), can be than ca lculated with the following expression [40]: \n\nwhere  b is the w idth of the Ti beam and  a is the debondlength. The encapsulation debond energy,  Gc, is then \n\nde fi ned as the value of  G at the onset of debonding, where  P = P c.\n\nThe value of  C for the SCB s pecimen is also a function  of the encapsulation stiffness, which dep ends on  T and \n\nthe  mechanical loading -rate [41]. Using the DTS system  described in Section 3.3, the value of  C (m/N) was \n\nmeasured at selected values of  a at 20 and 50° C in a chamber  of cont rolled RH (38%). The compliance data \n\nwere then  fi tted to the complia nce equation for a cantilever beam with  elastic foundation [42]: \n\nwhere  E, h, and  G are the Young ’s modulus, thickness, and  shear modulus of the Ti beam; and  B is an \n\nencapsulationdependent parameter given by \n\nwhere  t, ν, and  EE are the thickness, Poisson ’s ratio, and  modulus of the encapsulation, respectively. In the \n\ncase of  very large  a or very thin elastic beams ( h/a ≪ 1),  Eqn 3  takes the form of the compliance equation for \n\nthe elastic  beam in cantilever without encap sulation [43] present,  where  C = 4 a3/( Ebh 3). \n\nMaximizing elastic beam de fl ection (thinnest beam)  reduces the contribution of the encapsulation layer to \n\nthe  overall SCB compliance, improves the sensitivity of the  debond experiment, and reduces the size of th e\n\ndamage  zone ahead of the debond -tip. We therefore selected the  smallest possible value of  h (3.17 mm here) \n\nfor the Ti  beam —corresponding to a maximum bend ing stress of  ~70% the yield strength of Ti. \n\n2.3. SCB debond test displacement rate \n\nThe debond -tip  displacement rate,  δ_ , which characterizes  the deformation rates at the debond -tip, was \n\nkept constant  at every debonding cycle to minimize rate -effects in the  debond energy measurement. To keep \n\nδ_ constant, we  derived an equation for the required actuator  displacement  rate ( Δ_ ) as a function of  a as \n\nfollows.  The debond -tip opening displacement, δ, is given by where  ε y is the yield strain of the encapsulation. (At  debonding,  δ = δc and  G = Gc.) \n\nSubstituting Eqns 2 and 3 into the time -derivative of  Eqn 5,  and neglecting second -order and higher -order \n\nterms, the actuator displacement rate  Δ_ (m/s) required  to keep  δ_ constant (at debonding) at increasing \n\ndebondlengths can be approximated as \n\nwhere  Δ_ 0 is the reference actuator displacement rate (at a  refer ence debond -length  a0). To illustrate the use \n\nof this  equation, we calculated the value of  δ_ (at debonding) as a  function of  a, using Eqns 2, 3, and 5 with a \n\nrepresentative  SCB specimen (Figure 2). The values of  δ_ and  a were normalized to the initial val ues,  δ0̇ and \n\na0 = 15  mm, respectively. The value of  δ_ decreases rapidly with  a when the  SCB is loaded at a constant \n\ndisplacement rate ( Δ_ 0) but remains approximately constant ( δ_=δ_ 0≈1) when loaded at the  corrected \n\ndisplacement rate ( Δ_ ) given by Eqn 6 .\n\nIn thinner and st iffer encapsulation layers, where  tE / EE ≪ h, or when the debond experiment starts at large \n\ninitial debond -lengths ( h0.75 /a0 ≪ 1), Eqn 6 reduces to \n\nwhich has been previously used to determine the actuator  displacement rates in debond e xperiments with \n\nthinner  and stiffer  fi lms [10,13] \n\n2.4. Debond growth rate kinetics \n\nA detailed explanation of the load -relaxation method for  debond growth measurement  is provided \n\nelsewhere  [12,39]; a brief summary is given here. To measure the  encapsulation  debond growth rate, d a/ dt,\n\nas a function of  G, the SCB specimens were mechanically loaded in the  DTS adhesion test system (Section 3.2) \n\nup to a value of  G close to t he encapsulation debond energy. The load  relaxation was then recorded as a \n\nfunction of tim e for about  24 h at a  fi xed actuator displacement. Post -experiment  analysis of the \n\nload -relaxation data determined the debond  growth rates, d a/ dt, over the range of ~ 10 8 to ~10 4 m s 1 as a \n\nfunction of  G. The experiments were performed on new  encapsulation s pecimens in an environmental \n\nchamber at  selected values of RH (10, 25, 40, 55, and 70%) and  T (20, 30, 40, and 50° C). The load cell and \n\nactuator were  allowed to equil ibrate in the chamber environment for  6–8 h before each experiment. \n\nThe debond path in the  debond growth experiments  shifted occasionally from the EVA/glass to the EVA/Ti \n\ninterface (and vice versa), which limited the yield of the  debond growth experiments described in the \n\npreceding paragraphs. In a separate set of experiments, a temperature gra dient  was established on th e EVA layer, which successfully  stabilized the debond path at the warmer EVA/glass interface. To establish the  T\n\ngradient, the glass side of the SCB  specimen was heated on a  “hot -plate ” at a  fi xed temperature  (48, 53, or \n\n58° C) an d the Ti side was expose d to the colder  air of an environmental chamber (20, 24, or 30° C). The \n\nheat -fl ow required to maintain this temperature gradient  (4.1 kJm 2) was ~4 times that of solar illumination \n\n(1 kJm 2) as calculated with a one -dimension Fourier h eat  fl ow model using the rmal conductivities of 22, 1, \n\nand  0.23W m 1 K1 for Ti, glass, and EVA [44], respectively.  The purpose of this experiment was not to emulate \n\nfi eldconditions but to investigate the effect of a temperature gradient on stabilizing the de bonding path to \n\nthe EVA/ glass interface. The temperatures at the glass/EVA (28.4, 32.5, and  38° C) and EVA/Ti (20.3, 24.4, \n\nand 30.3° C) were also  calculated with this model. The surface of the hotplate and  the sides of the SCB \n\nspecimen were covered with a 5 -mm  sheet of polystyrene  foam to minimize heat loss. The hot  plate, SCB \n\nspecimen, and the delaminator system were  allowed to stabilize in the environmental chamber for one  hour \n\nto establish the  T gradient across the glass/EVA/Ti  structure. Once the gradient  of ~8° C was established , the \n\ndebond growth experiment described in the preceding paragraph was conducted. \n\n2.5. Tensile specimens \n\nThe SCB mechanical compliance and optimal SCB  testing displacement rate (employed to characterize d a/ dt\n\nand  G c) depend on the  value of the encapsulation modulus  (E)  as described in Sections 3.2 and 3.3. In order \n\nto fabricate  tensile experiments for module measurement, a 0.45 -mm  layer of EVA was laminated between \n\ntwo glass substrates.  Several 3.5 -mm square specimen s were then cut  from the  laminated structure with a \n\nhigh -speed precision saw to make  glass/EVA/glass tensile specimens (3.5 × 3.5 × 10.5 mm). To  measure the \n\nvalue of  E of the EVA layer, a loading tab was  attached to each glass substrate and a monotonic loa d was \n\napplied f rom 0.5 to +0.5MPa in an environmental chamber of controlled relative humidity (38%). The \n\nmodulus  was measured at selected values of  T in recently laminated  specimens (10 days) and in specimens \n\naged for 1 year in a  laboratory environment (23 °C,  fl uorescent  indoor illumination of ~200 lux, and RH \n\nranging from 25 to 45%). The  cross -section area of the tensile specimen corresponded to a  mixed state of \n\nplane -stress (edges) and plane -strain (center)  in the EVA layer. \n\n# 3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION \n\n3.1. Environmental  sensitivity of EVA  modulus \n\nThe elastic modulus of the EVA encapsulation ( E) as a  function of testing temperature,  T, is shown in Figure \n\n3 for recently laminated (10 days) and laboratory -aged  (1 year) specimens. Aging was conducted at 2 3° C, \n\nunder  fl uorescen t indoor illumination of ~200 lux, and 25 to  45% RH. The storage modulus of EVA,  EDMA ,\n\nmeasured  with dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) in a previous  experiment by one of the authors [45], is \n\nalso included.  The value of  E and  EDMA  decreased exponentially wi th  T in the entropy -elastic temperature \n\nrange (30 –60° C) below  the melting temperature (65° C),  Tm. The value of  E increased ~10 -fold with aging, \n\nwhich  was attributed to  thermal and possible photochemical cross -linking of EVA  [4] although the aged \n\nspecimens  were exposed only to  indoor illumination ( fl uorescent). To explain, recently  laminated EVA \n\nencapsulation may still contain as much  as 40% of the init ial peroxide used to facilitate cross -linking  [47,48]. \n\nIn  fi elded PV modules, the combination of residual  peroxide and UV radiation explains the cross -linking of \n\nEVA to up to ~90% gel content. \n\nThe Arrhenius equation was  fi tted to the inverse of the  meas ured moduli ( E1 and  EDMA 1) as shown in Figure \n\n3 in dotted lines ( T < Tm), with an activation energy of ~37  kJ  mol 1 in the new specimens and 40kJ mol 1 in \n\nthe aged  specimens, respectively. The similarity between the  activation energies suggests that aging does \n\nnot affect signi fi cantly the temperature -dependence of the compliance  over the one year age. Similarly, the \n\nmelting temperature,  Tm (65° C) [41] did not change signi fi cantly. At  T > Tm, where the polymer is in th e melt \n\nstate, the values of  EDMA  (1.1 MPa) and  E (0.7 and 4 MPa) were independent of  T.3.2. Mechanical compliance of SCB debond  specimen \n\nThe mechanical  compliance,  C, of the SCB specimen as a  function of debond length ( a) is shown in Figure 4 \n\nfor  EVA (circle data points) and PVB (square data points)  specimens, at two selected values of  T. The \n\ncompliance of  the bare Ti cantilever beam is also shown for re ference as  a solid line. The value of  C increased \n\nwith  a in the SC B specimens and in the bare Ti beam. The specimen with  the lowest encapsulation storage \n\nmodulus, PVB ( G′~0.4  to 40 MPa) [49], exhibited the largest SCB compliance,  followed by the specimen with \n\nEVA (G ′~20 to  1000 MPa) [41], followed by the bare Ti beam. The  value of  C increased with  T as a \n\nconsequence of the  temperature -dependence of the encapsulation modul us;  this dependence was stronger \n\nin the more compliant  encapsulation (PVB) \n\nTo obtain an analytical exp ression of  C as a function of  a, we  fi tted Eqn 3 to the compliance data of the SCB \n\nspecimens with EVA (dashed -line) and PVB (dotted -line),  as shown in Fig ure 4. The  fi tted equations were \n\nthen used  to calculate the encapsulation debond energy (Eqn 2) as  des cribed in Section 3.2. \n\n3.3. Encapsulation debond energy \n\nThe measured debond energy of the EVA/glass interface,  G c, decreased from 2.15 to 1.8 kJ m 2 in th e 25 to \n\n50° C temperature range (Figure 5). At temperatures close to  Tm (65° C), the value of  Gc decreased six -fold to \n\n0.3 kJ m 2. The value of  G c remained constant in the melt state at  any  T, similar to the modulus in Figure 3. \n\nThe debond energy of the PVB/ Ti interface, shown in  Figure 5 (circular data points), also decreased with  T\n\nin  the 20 to 40° C temperature range. The values of  Gc for  PVB/Ti (0.67 to 0.52 kJ m 2) were much lower than \n\nthose  of EVA/glass in the same temperature range, which can be  attribut ed to the lower yield stress of PVB \n\nand to the nature  of the attachment at the  interface. EVA is typically bonded  to glass covalently, as enabled \n\nthrough a silane chemistry  (primer) typically present in the EVA formulation[27]. In  contrast, PVB is more \n\nwea kly bonded via hydrogen  bonding [27]. \n\nTo better understand the effect of te mperature on  debond energy, Eqn 5 can be solved for  Gc (J m 2) [9]: \n\nwhere  E(t, T) is the temperature -dependent modulus of the  material at the debonding -tip. \n\nIn the EVA/glass interf ace, the extent of plastic deformation during debonding is determined  "
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4086:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:345:      "content": "Title: The Natural and Accelerated Evolution of EVA Adhesion Through Intermediate Exposures\n\nURL Source: https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/1887551\n\nNumber of Pages: 6\n\nMarkdown Content:\nThe Natural and Accelerated Evolution of EVA \n\n# Adhesion through Intermediate Exposures \n\nPatrick Thornton , Student Member, IEEE , Nick Bosco , and Reinhold H. Dauskardt \n\nAbstract —Ethylene vinyl acetate (EVA ) encapsulants comprise \n\nthe majority of the encapsulants currently in use ; much work has \n\nbeen done to understand and model the adhesive characteristics of \n\nEVA -encapsulated modules, but limited work has provided \n\nreliable insight into adhesion during the intermediate stages of \n\nexposur e, limiting the ability to validate model predictions in this \n\nrange. Here we provide the adhesion energy measurements for \n\nEVA adhesion after nearly 6 years of field aging and 10,000 hours \n\nof accelerated agi ng. Both field and accelerated aging reveal a \n\ndistinct plateau that emerges during the intermediate exposure \n\nperiods (after 1 year in the field and after 1,000 hours in a \n\nchamber). At 10,000 hours, adhesion within accelerated aged \n\nmini -modules falls to a l evel generally seen after long -term field \n\nexposure s (>15 years). Previous model ing predicted that adhesion \n\nwould steadily decrease over the lifetime of a module, but these \n\ncurrent results uncover an intermediate plateauing trend that is \n\nimportant to accura tely modeling the evolution of adhesion and \n\npredicting adhesive failure. Based on these findings , three key \n\nmodel refinements concerning the rate of UV -radical formation \n\nand subsequent ß -scission, the rate and acceleration of hydrolytic \n\ndepolymerization, and the profile of the plasticity contribution \n\nover time are implemented and discussed .\n\nKeywords —adhesion , EVA , field / accelerated agin g, modeling \n\nI. INTRODUCTION \n\nEncapsulation plays a fundamental role in all photovoltaic \n\n(PV) modules. Not only does it physica lly and electrically \n\nisolate the solar cell within , but it also adhesive ly couples the \n\noutermost module layers (glass and/or polymer backsheets) to \n\nthe solar cell . Ethylene vinyl acetate (EVA) has predominately \n\nfulfilled solar encapsulant needs since the 1980s [1] on account \n\nof its adequate material properties, including adhesion, \n\ncombined with its affordability [2] . However, with extensive \n\nexposure to heat, humidity, and UV radiation comes chemical \n\ndegradation [3] , which reduces its adhesive strength . This can \n\nultimately lead to delamination failure of the module. \n\nTo understand the evolution of adhesion within EVA -\n\nencapsulated modules, studies hav e offered controlled \n\ninvestigations that are mostly confined to short exposures ,\n\nwhich are obviously limited in their ability to inform on how \n\nadhesion loss progresses throughout a module’s lifetime [4] –\n\nTh is work was authored in part by the National Renewable Energy \n\nLaboratory, operated by Alliance for Sustainable Energy, LLC, for the U.S .\n\nDepartment of Energy (DOE) under Contract No. DE -AC36 -08GO28308. \n\nFunding provided by U.S. Department of Energy Office of Energy Efficiency \n\nand Renewable Energy Solar Energy Technologies Office. Corresponding \n\nauthor: R. H. Dauskardt \n\nPatrick Thornton is w ith Stanford University, Stanford, CA 94305 USA \n\n(email: pthornto@stanford.edu). \n\n[7] . Conversely, other non -controlled studies have provided \n\ninsights into the final state of adhesion in modules that have \n\nbeen in the field for long periods; however, due to the lack of a \n\ncontrol , the limitations of having only a singular , ultimate data \n\npoint, and variations in material utilized across module \n\nmanufacturers , these observational studies remain limited in \n\ntheir ability to detail how the evolution evolved over years of \n\nexposure [8] –[10] .\n\nHere we investigate the evolution of adhesion in EVA -\n\nencapsula ted mini -modules in a controlled study that includes \n\nspecimens aged for 10,000 hours in accelerated conditions and \n\nspecimens exposed in three different outdoor locations \n\n(Arizona, Colorado, and Florida) for nearly 6 years. The length \n\nof these controlled exposures provides reliable data for \n\nunderexplored intermediate times . The adhesion energ y was \n\nmeasured using a fracture mechanics -based , width -tapered \n\ncantilever beam test , which yields a material property , allowing \n\na more fundamental and quantitative assessment of adhesion .\n\nThe results add much -needed insight into the intermediate \n\nevolution of adhesive degradation and the path the adhesion \n\nloss takes from pristine to delaminated, which is an especially \n\ncrucial consideration for efforts seeking to model and predict \n\nadhesion over the course of a module’s lifetime [10], [11] .\n\nRecently, we developed a model framework to describe the \n\nmolecular events that result in adhesive degradation within \n\nEVA [10] . It demonstrated a strong ability to describe both \n\nshort -term (<1 year) a nd long -term (>15 years) data ; however, \n\nwithout any natural or accelerated results in the intermediate \n\nrange, a prediction of a continuous decrease emerged here that \n\nremained unverifiable because of the lack of intermediate data. \n\nWith the data and results from the current work, a trend emerges \n\nover intermediate exposure time s, providing the basis to refine \n\nthe model parameters. The refinement s include adjusting the \n\nra tes of the relevant degradation mechanisms (UV -radical \n\nformation/ß -scission and hydrolytic depolymerization) as well \n\nas updating the initial, simplifying assumption regarding the \n\nunderlying contribution associated with non -linear plasticity ,\n\nultimately all owing the model to more accurately capture the \n\nadhesion trends demonstrated in both field and accelerated \n\naging. This approach to characterizing and modeling adhesion \n\nis valuable to encapsulants beyond EV A since specific \n\nNick Bosco is with the National Renewable Energy Laboratory, Golden, CO \n\n80401 USA (email: nick.bosco@nrel.gov). \n\nReinhold H. Dauskardt is with the Materials Science and Engineering \n\nDepartment, Stanford University, Stanford, CA 94305 USA (email: \n\ndauskardt@stanford. edu ). Pursuant to the DOE Public Access Plan, this document represents the authors' peer-reviewed, accepted manuscript. The published version of the article is available from the relevant publisher. degradation mechanisms can be incor porated or removed as \n\nneeded, on account of the fundamental and modular nature of \n\nthe model. For example, with polyolefin elastomer (POE) \n\nencapsulants that are rapidly increasing in market share, the \n\ndeacetylation reaction would no longer be applicable, but the \n\nUV -radical formation, ß-scission, and hydrolytic \n\ndepolymerization reactions would be, and the model could be \n\nadjusted accordingly to account for this. \n\nII. EXPERIMENTAL DETAILS \n\nA. Mini -Module Preparation \n\nSingle cell (150 mm x 150 mm) PV m ini -modules were \n\nencapsulated with EVA (33% vinyl acetate by mass, 14% \n\nmolar, PhotoCap 15585P UF/HLT, Specialized Technology \n\nResources, Inc.) between a 3 mm borosilicate front glass and a \n\nKynar®/ PETE /EV A backsheet . Indoor aging consisted of a \n\n‘light’ and a ‘dark’ condition: light’ – 65˚C and 30% relative \n\nhumidity (RH) under 340 nm UV light (Atlas Ci4000 Weather -\n\nOmeter®, 81 W/m2, ~1.4x UV suns), and ‘dark’ – 85˚C and \n\n13.5% RH without UV light (Blue M, Mod el AC -7620HB \n\nchamber). The ‘light’ condition parameters were selected to \n\ncompensate for heating effects from the UV light, allowing for \n\naccurate comparisons between the two sets of conditions. \n\nOutdoor field aging occurred at 3 locations: Golden, Colorado \n\n(CO); Miami, Florida (FL); and Phoenix, Arizona (AZ). \n\nB. Adhesion Testing \n\nWidth -tapered cantilever beam tests were used to \n\ncharacterize the front encapsulant adhesion [12], [13] and \n\nmeasure the critical strain energy release r ate, or adhesion \n\nenergy, Gc [J/m 2]. First, the backsheet and rear encapsulant \n\nlayers are mechanically removed before a triangular beam \n\nspecimen (Ti, 6% Al, 4% V, 20° apex angle) is adhered to the \n\nrear side of the solar cell with a two -part epoxy (DP420 or \n\nDP8005 -black , 3M) . A load applied with a strain rate of 10 \n\nμm/s is placed at the apex of the triangle, which propagat es a\n\ncrack along the weakest interface at a critical load, Pc. The taper \n\nin the beam allows the crack to propagate at a constant load . Gc\n\nis evaluated with (1) , usin g the critical load and a measurement \n\nof the final crack length, a. The load line displacement at the \n\nbeam tip at the point of measurement is Δ, and θ is the beam \n\ntaper angle. \n\n𝐺 𝑐 = 𝑃 𝑐 ∆\n\n2𝑎 2 tan (θ/2) (1) \n\nIn this work, several crack lengths and associated critical \n\nloads are obtained by inspection through the front glass ; thus ,\n\nthe Gc values and error represent the mean value and standard \n\ndeviation of at least three measurements. \n\nIII. ANALYTICAL ADHESION MODEL \n\nThe original analytical model referenced throughout and \n\nrefined within this work is described in detail elsewhere [10] ;\n\nhowever, an overview is included here to provide a basis for the \n\nrefinements . The model attribute d the overall adhesion e nergy \n\nGc(t) [J/m 2] at a given time t [hours] to the sum of the intrinsic \n\nadhesion energy Gc,int,i (t) and contributions from localized \n\nplasticity Gc,PL (t) , given by \n\n𝐺 𝑐 (𝑡 ) = ∑ 𝐺 𝑐 ,𝑖𝑛𝑡 ,𝑖 (𝑡 ) + 𝐺 𝑐 ,𝑃𝐿 (𝑡 )3 \n\n> 𝑖 =1\n\n. (2)\n\nDecreases in Gc,int,i and Gc,PL are modeled in terms of \n\nmolecular degradation processes occurring in the EVA and are \n\nrelated to deacetylation, hydrolytic depolymerization, and bond \n\nscission associated with midchain radical generation and ß -\n\nscission . The se three chemical degradation proc esses are \n\ndescribed with four rate coefficients – k1 for deacetylation , k2\n\nfor hydrolytic depolymerization, and k3 and k4 for bond scission \n\nwhich consists of midchain radical generation (tied to k3) and \n\nß-scission (tied to k4).\n\nGc,int,i over the course of exposure is related to the \n\ndegradation rate as shown in (3), where Gc,int,i (0) is the initial \n\nadhesion energy for a given process, ai is an inten sity factory, \n\nand bi(t) to bi(0) are the number of remaining bonds and the \n\ninitial number of bonds susceptible to rupture , respectively ; t he \n\nbond ratio is a function of the rate coefficients discussed above .\n\n𝐺 𝑐 ,𝑖𝑛𝑡 ,𝑖 (𝑡 ) = 𝐺 𝑐 ,𝑖𝑛𝑡 ,𝑖 (0) ∗ exp (−𝑎 𝑖 (𝑏 𝑖 (𝑡 )\n\n> 𝑏 𝑖 (0)\n\n)) (3)\n\nGc,PL is proportional to Gc,int,i as shown in (4). The \n\nproportionality factor , d, at time t was approximated to be ( 5), \n\nincorporating a simplifying assumption where d decrease s\n\nlinearly with time. d(0) is a nonadjustable parameter that is a \n\nfunction of other input parameters , and t ∞ is the time at which \n\nthe Gc,PL contribution is negligible with respect to the initial \n\nvalue (~30 years). \n\n𝐺 𝑐 ,𝑃𝐿 (𝑡 ) = ∑ 𝐺 𝑐 ,𝑖𝑛𝑡𝑟 ,𝑖 (𝑡 ) ∗ 𝑑 (𝑡 )3 \n\n> 𝑖 =1\n\n(4)\n\n𝑑 (𝑡 ) = 𝑑 (0) ∗ (1 − 𝑡 \n\n> 𝑡 ∞\n\n) (5)\n\nIV. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION \n\nA. Field and Accelerated Testing \n\nFig. 1 juxtaposes the measured Colorado ( CO ) data with the \n\nmodel predictions specifically tuned with the 6 - and 12 -month \n\ndata and a 16 -year datum from Colorado [10] . When the \n\noriginal model was published, the reference data available \n\nthrough 12 months and beyond 15 years validated the model’s \n\npredictive capabilities. Other worldwide adhesion data largely \n\nsupported the overall long -term trends [10] . However, within \n\nthe current work that extends further into the intermediate \n\nexposure range (>1 year), a large devi ation emerges at 46 \n\nmonths between the measured (1510 J/m 2) and predicted value \n\n(~840 J/m 2). \n\nIn Fig. 2, beginning with an initial Gc of 1800 +/ - 80 J/m 2, the \n\ndata up through 12 months demonstrate a decreasing trend. \n\nHowever, from 12 to 68 months of exposure for these 3 outdoor \n\nlocations, a general plateauing of Gc emerges. After 68 months ,\n\nthe adhesion energies are 67% and 80% of initial for the \n\nArizona (AZ ) and Florida ( FL ) specimens , respectively, and Pursuant to the DOE Public Access Plan, this document represents the authors' peer-reviewed, accepted manuscript. The published version of the article is available from the relevant publisher. Fig. 1 Measured adhesion energy from CO mini -modules in comparison with     \n\n> predicted values from a model originally tuned with the 6 -and 12 -month and\n> 16 -year values (solid circles) . The model predicted a steadily decreasing trend\n> after the initial year –a trend not followed by the subsequently measured data\n> (open circles). Other prior worldwide adhesion data included for reference.\n\nafter 46 months, the adhesion energy is about 85% of the initial \n\nvalue for the CO specimen. \n\nAdditionally, the existence of a plateau during the \n\nintermediate exposure times is corroborated by the accelerated \n\naging data. Fig. 3 includes adhesion data for mini -modules aged \n\nfor 10,000 hours in both ‘ligh t’ and ‘dark ’ conditions . The \n\nresults from ‘light’ exposures, which include the same primary \n\nstressors as field exposures (heat, humidity, and UV radiation), \n\ndisplay a similar drop in the early onset of aging before \n\nanalogously plateauing at around 1550 J/ m2 up through 5,000 \n\nhours. By 10,000 hours, however, the adhesion energy \n\nconsiderably drops to 240 J/m 2, which is 14% of initial \n\nadhesion. This value is much closer in magnitude to the \n\nthreshold value of ~160 J/m 2 below which delamination failure \n\nis more l ikely to occur [8] . Additionally, this large drop in \n\nadhesio n energy is accompanied by a simultaneous shift in \n\nfailure interface from the EVA/cell to glass/EVA interface – a\n\nshift seen previously in fielded modules after significant \n\ndegradation/exposure [9], [12] . In ‘dark’ conditions, without \n\nthe UV radiation to help facilitate adhesive degradation, it takes \n\nlonger for the adhesion to experience that initial drop, and then \n\nit plateaus around 1600 J/m 2 through 10,000 hours. \n\nUnder ‘light’ conditions, the UV dosage produces \n\napproximately a 5.6X acceleration factor, considering the \n\naverage UV light for a fixed plate module is about 0.25 UV \n\nsuns. Then, assuming the sample temperature to be about 30˚C \n\nhigher than the average effective temperature of a fielded \n\nmodule, which accelerates the UV p rocesses by 1.4x for every \n\n10˚C, the overall acceleration is roughly calculated to be 15.4X \n\n(see Table I) . Based on this acceleration factor, 5,000 hours in \n\n‘light’ conditions approximates 8.5 years in the field, and \n\n10,000 hours approximates 17 years. Thu s, t he large drop in \n\nadhesion between 5,000 and 10,000 hours parallels what is \n\noften seen in field aged modules , including in those discussed \n\nabove and in [10] . Furthermore, based on the se accelerated \n\nresults, the plateau in the field aged modules could potentially \n\ncontinue for at least a few more years beyond the ~6 years                \n\n> Fig. 2Adhesion energy results for mini -modules aged outdoors in CO (blue),\n> AZ (orange), and FL (grey) through up to 68 months of exposure. Over the first\n> 12 months, adhesion decreases by 15 -20% , but from 12 through 68 months, the\n> adhesion energy values generally level off .Original and revised predictions are\n> superimposed on the data. Guide lines included to show plateauing trends.\n> Fig. 3Adhesion energy results for mini -modules aged in environmental\n> chambers under ‘light’ and ‘dark’ accelerated conditions. For ‘light’ conditions,\n> similar to outdoor results (see Fig. 2), a plateau through 5,000 hours emerges\n> after an initial drop in adhesi on energy, which is followed by a large decrease\n> measured at 10,000 hours. Guide lines included to show general trends.\n\nexplored in this study. Moreover, the alignment of accelerated \n\ndata with their fielded counterparts supports the use of \n\naccelerated agin g studies to probe these intermediate trends that \n\nare often challenging and time -consuming to address through \n\nfielded explorations. Pursuant to the DOE Public Access Plan, this document represents the authors' peer-reviewed, accepted manuscript. The published version of the article is available from the relevant publisher. TABLE I \n\nACCELERATION FACTOR CALCULATION \n\nStressor \n\nTypical Field \n\nAmount \n\nAccelerated \n\nAmount \n\nAccelerat ion \n\nFactor          \n\n> UV 0.25 UV suns 1.4 UV suns 5.6x\n> Heat 55˚C 85˚C 2.7x\n> Combined ----15.4x\n\nB. Model Refinements \n\nWithin the intermediate region, where the predicted adhesion \n\ndeviates from measured data, the critical factors that affect the \n\nmodel prediction are the rate of UV -radical formation and \n\nsubsequent ß -scission, the rate and acceleration of hydrolytic \n\ndepolymerizat ion, and the plasticity contribution. The recent \n\nintermediate data enable refinement of the parameters as \n\ndescribed below. Since the combination of the UV -radical \n\nformation and ß -scission reactions tend to dominate over the \n\nother reactions during this peri od [10], [14] , the current results \n\nsuggest that the rate of these reactions is slower than originally \n\npresumed. Thus, a decrease in the associated rate coefficients \n\nhelps delay the predicted adhesion loss – a 30% decrease is \n\nincluded in this revised model. This delay was also accounted \n\nfor by decreasing the intensity factor a3 from 1 to 0.75 and \n\ndoubling the intensity factor for deacetylation, a1, (in order to \n\npreserve the early adhesion drop ). R ecent work reveal s a\n\ncataly tic interaction between acetate salt buildup at the \n\nglass/EVA interface that would accelerate the normally very \n\nslow process of hydrolytic depolymerization [14] . The rate \n\ncoefficient for hydrolytic depolymerization originally included \n\na linear acceleration profile, but the results in [14] suggest that \n\nthe acceleration profil e should exhibit minimal change early on \n\nwhen the buildup of deacetylation pr oducts is low before more \n\nrapidly accelerating in the intermediate to late time frame as \n\nacetate species become more concentrated at the interface. \n\nFurther insight into the contribution from this mechanism \n\nsuggests that on account of the covalent siloxane bonds at the \n\ninterface, it should have a greater initial adhesion energy ,\n\nGc,int,2 (0) [15] – here, 50% of the initial intrinsic Gc instead of \n\n30% (with the initial Gc values from the other intrinsic \n\nprocesses reduced each by 10% to maintain the same overall \n\npercentage ). A summary of original and refined model \n\nparameters is included in Table I I.\n\nMost importantly, the plateau that emerge s through ~6 years \n\nreveal s that adjustments to the reaction rates alone can not \n\naccount for the leveling off of adhesion. More accurate \n\npredict ions require larger changes that can only arise fro m the \n\nplasticity contributions to G c. These can most effectively be \n\naddressed using the proportionality factor d, discussed in \n\nsection III . The linearly decreasing nature of d initially \n\nemployed is revised to reflect the plateauing in G c following an \n\ninitial decrease and the subsequent significant decrease beyond \n\n~6 years that is observed. These changes are better reflected \n\nusing an exponential function, as shown below: \n\n𝑑 (𝑡 ) = 𝑑 (0) ∗ exp ( 𝑡 \n\n> 𝑡 ∞\n\n). (5)   \n\n> Fig. 4The original and revised lifetime adhesion predictions alongside field\n> data over 30 years of exposure. The revised prediction captures the general data\n> trends throughout early, intermediate, and long -term exposure periods.\n\nThe revised and original predic tions through 10 years are \n\nsuperimposed on the field data in Fig. 2. The predicted adhesion \n\nloss now captures the plateauing trend observed in the \n\nintermediate region. Importantly, the prediction over the entire \n\nsolar module lifetime (~30 years) demonstrat es that with these \n\nrefinements, the model is still capable of capturing adhesion \n\ntrends in the early and late periods of exposure as shown in Fig. \n\n4. A few of the original data measured on modules from various \n\nworldwide locations deviate from the expected trends (such as \n\nthe low adhesion data at ~6 and ~11 years and those at ~27 \n\nyears), but this is to be expected on account of these assorted \n\nmodules having sometimes drastically differing bills of \n\nmaterials and the possibility for manufacturing/assembly \n\ndefe cts and other material variations . Notably, the overall shape \n\nof the prediction aligns with the general trends present in both \n\nfield and accelerated aging data, and the model remains \n\nsensitive and tunable to the location -specific environmental \n\ndifferences that would affect degradation rates. \n\nV. OUTLOOK AND CONCLUSION \n\nThrough both ~6 years of outdoor aging in 3 different US \n\nlocations and 5,000 hours of accelerated ‘light’ aging, a distinct \n\nplateau region forms following an initial drop in adhesion. \n\nSometime bet ween 5,000 and 10,000 hours of ‘light’ aging and \n\nafter ~6 years of field aging, adhesion energy drops to values \n\nan order of magnitude lower. The agreement between field and \n\naccelerated adhesion data demonstrates the effectivity and \n\npotential of utilizing a ccelerated testing schemes to investigate \n\nTABLE I I\n\nKEY MODEL PARAMETERS \n\nParameter  Original Value  Refined Value           \n\n> k12.60 x 10 -5[1/h] --\n> k2(0) 1.60 x 10 -12 [1/h] --\n> k31.25 x 10 -6[1/h] 8.75 x 10 -7[1/h]\n\nPursuant to the DOE Public Access Plan, this document represents the authors' peer-reviewed, accepted manuscript. The published version of the article is available from the relevant publisher. k4 8.90 x 10 -7 [1/h]  6.20 x 10 -7 [1/h] \n\nGc(0)  2000 [J/mm 2] 1800 [J/mm 2]\n\nGc,1 (0)  200 [J/mm 2] 120 [J/mm 2]\n\nGc,2 (0)  200 [J/mm 2] 300 [J/mm 2]\n\nGc,3 (0)  267 [J/mm 2] 180 [J/mm 2]\n\na1 5 [ -] 10 [ -]\n\na2 4 [ -] --\n\na3 1 [ -] 0.75 [ -]\n\nlonger -term trends much more quickly. Previous adhesive \n\nmodeling predicted that the adhesion energy would \n\ncontinuously decrease , which appeared accurate without \n\ninclusion of these recent intermediate data. \n\nTh ese results in combination with a powerful and fundamental \n\npredictive model enable a greater understand ing of the \n\nevolution and nature of adhesive degradation within EVA. By \n\naddressing the principal factors that affect the model prediction \n\nduring the intermediate exposure time frame – the rate of UV -\n\nradical formation and subsequent ß -scission, the rate and \n\nacceleration of hydrolytic depolymerization, and the plasticity \n\ncontribution – the refined model can now accurately capture \n\nadhesion trends throughout the entirety of a module’s lifetime. \n\nImportantly, this modeling framework and approach are \n\napplicable to encapsulants beyond EVA since the degradation \n\nmechanisms relevant to any given encapsulant can be \n\nsubstituted and incorporated into the model. Thus, t he insights \n\nprovided through this inve stigation not only highlight the \n\nimportance of studying intermediate adhesion trends but will \n\nalso prove invaluable to modeling the adhesive properties and \n\ndegradation of other solar encapsulants .\n\nACKNOWLEDGMENT \n\nWe thank Mike Kempe at NREL for his insight regarding \n\nestimation of acceleration factors. .\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4089:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:6418:      "full_text": "Title: Damage-Associated Molecular Patterns and Their Signaling Pathways in Primary Blast Lung Injury: New Research Progress and Future Directions\n\nURL Source: https://www.mdpi.com/1422-0067/21/17/6303/pdf?version=1598869065\n\nPublished Time: Mon, 31 Aug 2020 10:17:45 GMT\n\nNumber of Pages: 20\n\nMarkdown Content:\nInternational Journal of \n\n## Molecular Sciences Review \n\n# Damage-Associated Molecular Patterns and Their Signaling Pathways in Primary Blast Lung Injury: New Research Progress and Future Directions \n\nNing Li 1,2, †, Chenhao Geng 1,2, †, Shike Hou 1,2 , Haojun Fan 1,2, * and Yanhua Gong 1,2, * \n\n> 1\n\nInstitute of Disaster Medicine, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China; lining620@tju.edu.cn (N.L.); gengchenhao@tju.edu.cn (C.G.); houshike@tju.edu.cn (S.H.)  \n\n> 2\n\nTianjin Key Laboratory of Disaster Medicine Technology, Tianjin 300072, China \n\n* Correspondence: haojunfan86@126.com (H.F.); gongyanhua@tju.edu.cn (Y.G.) \n\n† Ning Li and Chenhao Geng contributed equally to this work. Received: 14 July 2020; Accepted: 20 August 2020; Published: 31 August 2020 \u0001\u0002\u0003\u0001\u0004\u0005\u0006\u0007\b \u0001\n\n> \u0001\u0002\u0003\u0004\u0005\u0006\u0007\n\nAbstract: Primary blast lung injury (PBLI) is a common cause of casualties in wars, terrorist attacks, and explosions. It can exist in the absence of any other outward signs of trauma, and further develop into acute lung injury (ALI) or a more severe acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). The pathogenesis of PBLI at the cellular and molecular level has not been clear. Damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) is a general term for endogenous danger signals released by the body after injury, including intracellular protein molecules (HMGB1, histones, s100s, heat shock proteins, eCIRP, etc.), secretory protein factors (IL-1 β, IL-6, IL-10, TNF-α, VEGF, complements, etc.), purines and pyrimidines and their derived degradation products (nucleic acids, ATP, ADP, UDPG, uric acid, etc.), and extracellular matrix components (hyaluronic acid, fibronectin, heparin sulfate, biglycan, etc.). DAMPs can be detected by multiple receptors including pattern recognition receptors (PRRs). The study of DAMPs and their related signaling pathways, such as the mtDNA-triggered cGAS-YAP pathway, contributes to revealing the molecular mechanism of PBLI, and provides new therapeutic targets for controlling inflammatory diseases and alleviating their symptoms. In this review, we focus on the recent progress of research on DAMPs and their signaling pathways, as well as the potential therapeutic targets and future research directions in PBLI. \n\nKeywords: primary blast lung injury; ALI; DAMPs; PRRs; signaling pathways \n\n1. Introduction \n\nExplosions often occur in modern wars, terrorist attacks, or daily emergencies, and blast injury is a common cause of casualties [ 1, 2]. At the moment of explosion, the explosive materials almost instantaneously convert from solid or liquid into gas. Gas expands rapidly from the explosion point and displaces the surrounding medium to create a blast overpressure (BOP), which transfers a large amount of energy to the surrounding medium to form the blast pressure wave [ 3]. Generally speaking, blast injuries can be divided into 4 categories, including primary (injury from direct e ff ect of blast pressure wave), secondary (fragmentation injury), tertiary (injury caused by bodily displacement or building collapse) and quaternary (includes burn injury and inhalation of toxic substances) [ 3–7]. Furthermore, some research proposed quinary injuries, but the specific content has not reached a consensus [ 3 ,5 , 6, 8]. In these types of injuries, primary blast injury (PBI) is a potentially life threatening, multi-system disease [ 4 ,8]. The previous common view was that blast waves mainly exert force at the air-tissue interfaces within the body. Therefore, organ systems with high air content, such as the auditory systems, pulmonary and gastrointestinal, are most likely a ff ected by PBI [ 3]. Among them,    \n\n> Int. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 ,21 , 6303; doi:10.3390 /ijms21176303 www.mdpi.com /journal /ijms\n\nInt. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 , 21 , 6303 2 of 20 \n\nlungs may be particularly susceptible to blast waves given tissue-density gradients [ 9]. Although recent studies indicated that blast-induced traumatic brain injury (bTBI) may be more common than previously believed [ 4 ,10 ], primary blast lung injury (PBLI) is still worthy of attention. The blast wave-induced acute lung injuries (ALI) are generally closed wounds rather than traditional penetrating wounds, which can be sustained in the absence of any other external signs of thoracic trauma [ 6]. However, it may further develop into acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), a more severe physiological expression of ALI [ 11 ]. Although the current research on the pathophysiological characteristics of PBLI has been relatively clear, the mechanisms at the cellular and molecular levels are still not fully understood. Damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) was first proposed by Land in 2003 [ 12 ]. It is the general term for endogenous risk signals released by the organism after injury, which can trigger non-infectious and uncontrolled inflammation in the body [ 13 , 14 ], aggravating the lung tissue damage after an explosion [ 15 ]. This review will focus on DAMPs and their signaling pathways related to PBLI, and explore possible therapeutic targets in future clinical treatment. \n\n2. Overview of Primary Blast Lung Injury \n\n2.1. The Mechanism of Primary Blast Lung Injury \n\nPBLI is mainly caused by the blast wave directly passing through the chest wall, which dissipates kinetic energy in the lung tissue and causes ALI [ 5 ]. In 1950, Schardin described three types of explosive forces that can cause injury: spallation, implosion, and inertia [ 16 ]. The model of normal alveolar capillaries is shown in Figure 1a. Spallation is defined as the displacement and fragmentation of the dense medium into a less dense medium (Figure 1b). Implosion is caused by the sharply reduced alveolar volume due to the high compression of air in the alveoli by overpressure wave, and then the alveoli re-expand rapidly by underpressure wave (Figure 1c). Inertia refers to the shear force produced by shock waves propagating at di ff erent speeds through di ff erent density tissues [ 3, 17 ,18 ]. These three forces work together to cause pulmonary hemorrhage and contusion, and once the air in the alveoli enters the pulmonary circulation, it may cause air embolism, resulting in a series of symptoms such as pulmonary edema, pneumothorax, and interstitial emphysema [ 19 ,20 ]. The description of these three injury mechanisms is based on the blast wave generated by open air explosions. The extent to which these forces actually cause damage is not completely clear [ 3 ]. Furthermore, research shows that the reflection of blast waves in confined spaces will significantly enhance their e ff ects and further aggravate the injury [18]. Int. J. Mol. Sci. 20 20 , 21, x FOR PEER REVIEW 3 of 23                                  \n\n> Figure 1. Damage mechanism of blast lung injury .(a) Normal alveolar capillaries .(b)\n> Schematic diagram of spallation . Alveolar hemorrhage causes the release of cytokines and\n> chemokines .(c) Schematic diagram of implosion . Air in the alveoli enters the pulmonary\n> circulation, causing air embolism.\n> 2.2. Pathophysiological Characteristics and Clinical Diagnosis of Primary Blast Lung Injury\n> The blast wave causes the rupture of alveolar capillaries, resulting in the presence of free\n> hemoglobin (Hb) and extrav asated blood in the lung tissue, induction of free radical reactions\n> that cause oxidative damage, initiation and augmentation of a pro -inflammatory response\n> [5,21] . Furthermore, leucocytes can be demonstrated at the hemorrhagic areas within 3 h after\n> blast exposure, and their levels increase at 24 h or more [22] . Histological examination reveal s\n> obvious perivascular edema and massive alveolar hemorrhage within the first 12 h, followed\n> by damage to epithelial cells (at 12 –24 h) and endothelial cells (at 24 –56 h)[23] .\n\nFigure 1. Damage mechanism of blast lung injury. ( a) Normal alveolar capillaries. ( b) Schematic diagram of spallation. Alveolar hemorrhage causes the release of cytokines and chemokines. ( c) Schematic diagram of implosion. Air in the alveoli enters the pulmonary circulation, causing air embolism. Int. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 , 21 , 6303 3 of 20 \n\n2.2. Pathophysiological Characteristics and Clinical Diagnosis of Primary Blast Lung Injury \n\nThe blast wave causes the rupture of alveolar capillaries, resulting in the presence of free hemoglobin (Hb) and extravasated blood in the lung tissue, induction of free radical reactions that cause oxidative damage, initiation and augmentation of a pro-inflammatory response [ 5, 21 ]. Furthermore, leucocytes can be demonstrated at the hemorrhagic areas within 3 h after blast exposure, and their levels increase at 24 h or more [ 22 ]. Histological examination reveals obvious perivascular edema and massive alveolar hemorrhage within the first 12h, followed by damage to epithelial cells (at 12–24 h) and endothelial cells (at 24–56 h) [23]. Clinically, patients with PBLI often have dyspnea, shallow and frequent breathing [ 24 ]. In chest imaging, PBLI appears as ground-glass opacity (GGO), consolidation, and bilateral flu ff y infiltrates that resemble a “butterfly” or “bat wing” [ 4 ,25 ]. In addition, alveolar hemorrhage, laceration of lung parenchyma, subcutaneous emphysema, pneumothorax, and hemothorax can also be observed [ 25 ,26 ]. \n\n2.3. The Medical Treatment of Primary Blast Lung Injury \n\nAfter an explosion incident, the emergency personnel should first identify whether there is the risk of PBLI according to the place where the explosion occurred and the distance between the wounded and the center of explosion [ 5 ]. It is imperative to perform pre-hospital first aid, and notify the relevant department sta ff in advance to make relevant preparations, so as to ensure the smooth progress of follow-up treatment [ 4]. The current in-hospital treatment strategies for PBLI mainly include fluid resuscitation, hyperbaric oxygen therapy, mechanical ventilation, etc. [ 27 –29 ], and can be combined with drugs that inhibit inflammatory factors, scavenge oxygen free radicals, relieve smooth muscle spasm, and improve lung ventilation [ 30 ]. These interventions aim to control the development of the disease and maintain the vital signs of patients. In addition, the application of new biotechnology such as mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the treatment of PBLI has become a research hotspot in recent years [ 31 ,32 ]. However, due to insu ffi cient research on the molecular mechanism of PBLI, there is no specific medicine for the treatment of PBLI. Therefore, the study of DAMPs in PBLI is of great significance for exploring potential therapeutic targets and developing targeted treatment methods. \n\n3. DAMPs and Their Sensing Receptors \n\n3.1. Damage-Associated Molecular Pattern \n\nThe immune system defends our body with the mechanism of “self versus non-self” recognition. However, it can be activated not only by exogenous “non-self” substances, but also by endogenous DAMPs [ 33 ]. DAMPs are numerous intracellular molecules with physiological functions, which exist in the nucleus, mitochondria, or cytoplasm [ 34 –36 ]. Under normal physiological conditions, DAMPs cannot be recognized by the immune system, but they are released when cells die or are subjected to stress [ 37 ,38 ]. When released into the extracellular environment, DAMPs can activate innate immune cells, such as polymorphonuclear neutrophils (PMNs), natural killer cells (NKs), macrophages, and dendritic cells (DCs), as well as non-immune cells, such as epithelial cells, endothelial cells, and fibroblasts [ 39 ]. The activation of these cells results in the release of various cytokines and chemokines, which further recruit inflammatory cells and activate adaptive immune responses. In addition, some DAMPs can directly activate adaptive immune cells to generate immune responses. Whereas the local inflammatory response plays an important role in tissue repair and regeneration, excessive or persistent inflammation may also lead to a systemic and uncontrolled inflammatory response inducing remote organ failure [ 40 ]. \n\n3.2. DAMP-Sensing Receptors \n\nDAMPs released by injured organs, tissues, or cells can be detected by multiple receptors including pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) (Figure 2), which can, in turn, activate multiple pathways to regulate the inflammatory response. Moreover, one DAMP may bind to a variety of receptors and participate in di ff erent pathways [40]. Int. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 , 21 , 6303 4 of 20 Int. J. Mol. Sci. 20 20 , 21, x FOR PEER REVIEW 5 of 23           \n\n> Figure 2. Relationship between DAMPs and their sensing receptors and the activation of\n> different types of cells. DAMPs are released from cells which are dead or stressed due to tissue\n> injury, and include intracellular protein molecules, secreted protein factors, pur ine and\n> pyrimidine molecules, and their derived degradation products (DDPs) and extracellular\n> matrix components. They activate downstream signaling pathways by recognizing their\n> sensing receptors to activate innate immune cells or non -immune cells, then ad aptive immune\n> cells, and also directly activate adaptive immune cells to initiate inflammatory response.\n> Different cellular populations communicate with each other through cytokines and\n> chemokines.\n\n3.2.1. Pattern Recognition Receptors Bound by Damage -Assoc iated Molecular Patterns \n\nPRRs are expressed by innate immune cells such as PMNs, NKs, macrophages , and DCs, \n\nand mainly include toll -like receptors (TLRs), NOD -like receptors (NLRs), retinoic acid -\n\ninducible gene -I (RIG -I) -like receptors (RLRs), C -type lectin receptors (CLRs) , and multiple \n\ncytoplasmic DNA sensors (CDSs) [41] . TLRs recognize DAMP molecules and participate in \n\ninflammatory response [42] . NLRs promote the activation of inflammatory bodies [43] . RLRs \n\nare one of the main receptor families for sensing viral RNA [44] . The main function of CLRs is \n\nto induce pro -inflammatory response [45] . CDSs play essential roles in the development of \n\ninflammatory diseases and tumors [46] .\n\n1. Toll -like receptors \n\nTLRs are located on the cell surface or inside the cell, and can recognize DAMPs such as \n\nnucleic acids, HMGB1, heat shock proteins (HSPs) , and S100 proteins. Most TLRs (excep t TLR3) \n\nare recruited by myeloid differentiation protein 88 (MyD88) -dependent IL -1 receptor -\n\nassociated kinase (IRAK), which then activates nuclear factor -κB (NF -κB) to promote   \n\n> Figure 2. Relationship between DAMPs and their sensing receptors and the activation of di ff erent types of cells. DAMPs are released from cells which are dead or stressed due to tissue injury, and include intracellular protein molecules, secreted protein factors, purine and pyrimidine molecules, and their derived degradation products (DDPs) and extracellular matrix components. They activate downstream signaling pathways by recognizing their sensing receptors to activate innate immune cells or non-immune cells, then adaptive immune cells, and also directly activate adaptive immune cells to initiate inflammatory response. Di ff erent cellular populations communicate with each other through cytokines and chemokines.\n\n3.2.1. Pattern Recognition Receptors Bound by Damage-Associated Molecular Patterns PRRs are expressed by innate immune cells such as PMNs, NKs, macrophages, and DCs, and mainly include toll-like receptors (TLRs), NOD-like receptors (NLRs), retinoic acid-inducible gene-I (RIG-I)-like receptors (RLRs), C-type lectin receptors (CLRs), and multiple cytoplasmic DNA sensors (CDSs) [ 41 ]. TLRs recognize DAMP molecules and participate in inflammatory response [ 42 ]. NLRs promote the activation of inflammatory bodies [ 43 ]. RLRs are one of the main receptor families for sensing viral RNA [ 44 ]. The main function of CLRs is to induce pro-inflammatory response [ 45 ]. CDSs play essential roles in the development of inflammatory diseases and tumors [46]. 1. Toll-like receptors TLRs are located on the cell surface or inside the cell, and can recognize DAMPs such as nucleic acids, HMGB1, heat shock proteins (HSPs), and S100 proteins. Most TLRs (except TLR3) are recruited by myeloid di ff erentiation protein 88 (MyD88) -dependent IL-1 receptor-associated kinase (IRAK), which then activates nuclear factor-κB (NF-κB) to promote inflammatory response by upregulating the Int. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 , 21 , 6303 5 of 20 \n\nexpression of tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), interleukin 6 (IL-6), and IL-8 [ 40 ,47 ]. TLR3 participates in the inflammatory response through TRIF dependent pathway [42]. 2. NOD-like receptors NLRs are PRRs located in the cytoplasm, which can recruit pro-caspase-1 to start the assembly of inflammatory bodies after being activated by DAMPs such as ATP and oxidized mitochondrial DNA (ox-mtDNA), thereby inducing apoptosis and secretion of IL-1 β, IL-18 and other cytokines through the activation of caspase-1 [43,48]. 3. Retinoic acid-inducible gene-I (RIG-I)-like receptors The RLR family includes RIG-I, MDA5, and LGP2, which can distinguish between exogenous RNA and endogenous RNA to resist pathogens [ 44 ]. It can sense DAMPs such as endogenous 5 ′ppp RNA and unedited long self-dsRNA in vivo , and promote the production of type I interferon (IFN-I) and other cytokines and chemokines [49,50]. 4. C-type lectin receptors Some members of the CLR family, such as macrophage-inducible C-type lectin (MINCLE) and dendritic cell natural killer lectin group receptor 1 (DNGR1), are related to the development of inflammation diseases after binding to DAMPs. DNGR1 is a DC receptor which can be activated by F-actin to downregulate the production of the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10 in DCs and aggravate the inflammatory response [ 51 ]. MINCLE binds to SAP130 protein to induce pro-inflammatory response [45]. 5. Cytoplasmic DNA sensors Cyclic GMP–AMP synthase (cGAS) and absent in melanoma 2 (AIM2) are two important DNA sensors in the cytoplasm, which play essential roles in the development of inflammatory diseases and tumors [ 52 ,53 ]. Some studies have shown that self-DNA, produced by immunogenic cell death or nuclear DNA damage, can activate AIM2 or cGAS and promote the production of IFN-I and other cytokines and chemokines [46]. 3.2.2. Other Receptors /Channels Bound by DAMPs In addition to PRRs, DAMPs can activate receptors including triggering receptors expressed on myeloid cells (TREMs), receptor for advanced glycation end products (RAGE), several G-protein -coupled receptors (GPCRs), and ion channels [ 41 ]. TREM activation causes cytokine secretion, leading to inflammation response. The interaction between 35 kDa transmembrane receptor RAGE and its ligands also leads to the activation of pro-inflammatory genes [ 54 ,55 ]. GPCRs are known as seven-(pass)-transmembrane receptors and involved in many diseases via the cAMP or the phosphatidylinositol signal pathway [ 56 ]. Ion channels trigger the production of NLRP3 inflammasome and participate in the inflammatory response [57]. 1. Triggering receptors expressed on myeloid cells TREM1 and TREM2 are cell surface innate immune receptors which act as members of the immunoglobulin variable (IgV) domain receptor superfamily [ 58 ]. TREM1 is expressed on myeloid cells, epithelial cells, endothelial cells, and fibroblasts, and interacts with DAMPs such as HMGB1, HSP70, and actin to promote the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines [ 59 , 60 ]. TREM2 is highly expressed in myeloid cells such as DCs, monocytes, and macrophages, and binds to DAMPs such as HSP60, apolipoproteins (APOJ and APOE), and low-density lipoprotein (LDL) to regulate cell di ff erentiation, phagocytosis, chemotaxis, and other processes, and participate in inflammatory diseases [61,62]. Int. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 , 21 , 6303 6 of 20 \n\n2. Receptor for advanced glycation end products RAGE is a transmembrane protein that belongs to the immunoglobulin receptor superfamily and is expressed by multiple cell types, including neutrophils, monocytes, smooth muscle cells, endothelial cells, and cancer cells, and mediates cell response to DAMPs such as HMGB1, S100 proteins, advanced glycation end products (AGEs), and DNA to promote the expression of pro-inflammatory genes, as well as cell migration, proliferation, and apoptosis [63]. 3. G-protein-coupled receptors Various DAMPs can also promote inflammation via GPCRs. The N-formyl peptide receptors (FPRs) can recognize endogenous N-formylated peptides and promote the chemotaxis of neutrophils and monocytes /macrophages [ 64 ]. P2Y receptors (P2YRs) can sense extracellular nucleotides and promote the migration and activation of multifarious immune cells [ 65 ]. Calcium-sensing receptors (CaSRs) and G-protein-coupled receptor family C group 6 member A (GPRC6A) can recognize extracellular Ca 2+,promote monocyte /macrophage recruitment and the activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome [66]. 4. Ion channels Transient receptor potential (TRP) channels and P2X receptors are two types of ligand-gated ion channels, which can promote the production of cytokines and chemokines and the activation of NLRP3 inflammasome by recognizing mitochondria-derived reactive oxygen species (ROS) and ATP, respectively, and participate in the inflammatory response [57,67]. \n\n3.3. Crosstalk Between DAMP and Their Sensing Receptors \n\nIn summary, DAMPs can bind to related receptors or channel proteins and activate downstream signaling pathways, leading to large-scale cytokine release, including IL-1, IL-6, IL-8, IL-12, TNF-α, and type I /\n\ntype II IFN. These mediators enhance the activation, maturation, proliferation, and recruitment of immune cells at the injured regions, leading to the indirect activation of innate and adaptive immune cells (such as DCs or T cells) (Figure 2). \n\n4. DAMPs and Their Signaling Pathways Related to PBLI \n\n4.1. Intracellular Protein Molecules \n\n4.1.1. Nuclear Protein HMGB1 HMGB1 is a late inflammatory mediator associated with sepsis, malignancy, and immune disease, and is one of the most intensively studied DAMPs. Levels of HMGB1 may reflect the severity of inflammation and tissue damage, indicating a potential role for HMGB1 as a biomarker of ALI, and a potential target for blocking inflammatory pathways [ 68 ]. Under normal physiological conditions, HMGB1 is located in the nucleus, but it is passively released from necrotic cells after PBLI. HMGB1 triggers signaling cascades by combining with RAGE, TLR2, and TLR4, thereby inducing the activation of the NF-κB pathway and participating in the inflammatory response [ 14 ](Figure 3). Li et al. [ 69 ] demonstrated that HMGB1 participates in ALI by activating double-stranded RNA-dependent protein kinase (PKR) in macrophages and inducing macrophage (M1) polarization via TLR2- and TLR4-mediated NF-κB signaling pathways in a mouse model of ALI induced by the bacterial endotoxin lipopolysaccharide (LPS). In addition, HMGB1 levels are also related to processes such as hyperfibrinolysis and complement activation and participate in systemic inflammatory responses [ 70 ]. Int. J. Mol. Sci. 2020 , 21 , 6303 7 of 20 Int. J. Mol. Sci. 20 20 , 21, x FOR PEER REVIEW 8 of 23                     \n\n> Figure 3. Potential DAMPs and related signaling pathways in BLI. They \n\nprovide structural stability to chromatin and regulate gene expression within the nucleus .\n\nHowever, once released from the nucleus into the cytoplasm or body fluids, histones can act as \n\na DAMP , activating the imm une system and causing further cytotoxicity. They interact with \n\nTLRs, complement and cell membrane phospholipids inducing endothelial and epithelial \n\ncytotoxicity, TLR2/TLR4/TLR9 activation and pro -inflammatory cytokine/chemokine release \n\nvia MyD88, NF -κB an d NLRP3 inflammasome -dependent pathways [71] (Figure 3). Abrams et \n\nal. The results showed that circulating \n\nhistones surged immediately a"
.\outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_15\codex_stderr.log:4105:outputs\polaris_graph\baseline_v2_materials\PG_LB_FS_02_materials.json:220:      "content": "Abstract In civil engineering, with the increasing demand for structural reinforcement and renovation projects, polymer-rich anchoring adhesives have attracted much attention due to their performance advantage of having high strength and have become a key factor in ensuring the safety and durability of buildings. In this study, polymer-rich anchoring adhesives underwent three artificial aging treatments (alkali medium, hygrothermal, and water bath) to evaluate their aging performance. Alkali treatment reduced bending strength by up to 70% (sample 5#) within 500 h before stabilizing, while hygrothermal and water-curing treatments caused reductions of 16–51% and 15–77%, respectively, depending on adhesive composition. Dynamic thermomechanical analysis revealed significant loss factor decreases (e.g., epoxy adhesives dropped from >1.0 to stable lower values after 500 h aging), indicating increased rigidity. Infrared spectroscopy confirmed chemical degradation, including ester group breakage in vinyl ester resins (peak shifts at 1700 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1) and molecular chain scission in unsaturated polyesters. The three test methods consistently demonstrated that 500 h of aging sufficiently captured performance trends, with alkali exposure causing the most severe degradation in sensitive formulations (e.g., samples 5# and 6#). These results can be used to establish quantitative benchmarks for adhesive durability assessment in structural applications. Keywords: polymer-rich anchoring adhesives, dynamic thermomechanical analysis, infrared analysis, bending test, alkali, hygrothermal aging 1. Introduction In civil engineering, structural reinforcement and renovation projects are crucial for ensuring the safety and durability of buildings. Adhesive anchors, as essential connection and reinforcement components, play a pivotal role in these projects [1,2]. In recent years, the utilization of polymer-rich anchoring adhesives in adhesive-type anchor bolts has seen a significant rise. These adhesives exhibit performance advantages such as high strength and excellent durability and are increasingly becoming a focal point for both research and application [3,4]. Polymer-rich anchoring adhesives have the advantages of traditional ones and show properties of rapid consolidation, low fluidity, high-temperature resistance, and low sensitivity to erosive environments [5,6,7]. Polymer-rich anchoring adhesives primarily consist of reactive resins, hardeners, quartz particles, and other inorganic materials [8,9,10]. Polymer-rich anchoring adhesives (such as modified epoxy resin adhesives, modified vinyl ester resin adhesives and non-vinyl ester unsaturated polyester resin adhesive) have exhibited exceptional performance across a wide range of civil engineering applications [11,12]. According to Anam Afzal’s research [13], polymer-rich anchoring adhesives not only possess the same good bonding performance as traditional anchoring adhesives, but their characteristic of a fast consolidation speed could also significantly shorten the construction period, their low fluidity is helpful for precise construction, their high-temperature resistance makes them suitable for structural reinforcement in high-temperature environments, and their low sensitivity to erosive environments enhances the durability of the structure under harsh conditions. In a bridge reinforcement project, A Al-Mosawe [14] pointed out that polymer-rich anchoring adhesives could effectively enhance the bridge structure’s connection strength, improve the structure’s overall bearing capacity, and prolong the service life of the bridge. The structural transformation of high-rise buildings could also provide reliable anchoring connections, thereby ensuring the stability and integrity of the structure [15]. The following methods were adopted to evaluate the performance of anchoring adhesives. The first method involved a bulk specimen test for adhesive material property testing, and a steel–steel bonded single-lap adhesive joint was subjected to a shear test [16,17]. The second method involved conducting a test after implanting screws or rebar into concrete and conducting a test after slicing and artificially aging the rebar-embedded specimens [18,19,20]. The former involved simple sample preparation and testing methods and could be used to quantitatively obtain various physical performance indicators of the adhesive. The conditions of the latter were closer to the actual anchoring application state and could be used to obtain qualitative conclusions, but sample preparation and testing were complex. Both methods have advantages and disadvantages. When the initial single-shear test method was applied to evaluate the durability of polymer-rich anchoring adhesives, interfacial failure (adhesive–steel bond breakdown) often preceded cohesive failure (internal adhesive fracture) due to the polymer’s properties. This premature interfacial failure skewed the test outcomes, preventing an accurate assessment of the adhesive’s true load-bearing capacity, as its structural integrity was not fully challenged.
